content:
# Disagreement Log -- Joshua Chapter 23

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 23:1-2 -- "advanced in days" vs. "well along in years" (ba ba-yamim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "advanced in days" |
| LOGOS | "advanced in days" |
| PNEUMA | "well along in years" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "advanced in days" -- EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew idiom ba ba-yamim literally. PNEUMA's "well along in years" communicates the meaning but loses the distinctive Hebrew phrasing. The idiom is comprehensible in English and recurs as a fixed formula for aging leaders throughout the Hebrew Bible (cf. Gen 18:11, 24:1; Josh 13:1). The consensus follows the majority.

---

### 23:2 -- "summoned" vs. "called" vs. "called together" (va-yiqra)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And Joshua called to all Israel" |
| LOGOS | "Joshua summoned all Israel" |
| PNEUMA | "Joshua called together all of Israel" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Joshua summoned all Israel" -- The Hebrew va-yiqra can mean "called" or "summoned." In context, Joshua is formally convening the entire national leadership for a farewell address, not casually calling out. "Summoned" (LOGOS) best captures this official, deliberate act.

---

### 23:3 -- "has been fighting" vs. "fought" vs. "the one fighting" (ha-nilcham)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he [is] the one fighting for you" |
| LOGOS | "is the one who fought for you" |
| PNEUMA | "is the one who has been fighting for you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "is the one who has been fighting for you" -- The Hebrew ha-nilcham is a Niphal participle, expressing characteristic or ongoing action rather than a completed past event. PNEUMA's present perfect progressive tense ("has been fighting") best represents the participial aspect -- YHWH's warfare has been continuous throughout the conquest. The relative clause structure follows LOGOS for readability.

---

### 23:4 -- Territorial description: syntax and "toward the setting of the sun" vs. "in the west"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "I have allotted to you these nations, the remaining [ones], [as] an inheritance for your tribes, from the Jordan, and all the nations that I have cut off, and the Great Sea [toward] the setting of the sun." |
| LOGOS | "I have allotted to you these remaining nations as an inheritance for your tribes, from the Jordan, and all the nations that I have cut off, to the Great Sea toward the setting of the sun." |
| PNEUMA | "I have assigned to you by tribe all the remaining nations as your inheritance, from the Jordan all the way to the Great Sea in the west, including every nation I have already destroyed." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "I have allotted to you these remaining nations as an inheritance for your tribes, from the Jordan, and all the nations that I have cut off, to the Great Sea toward the setting of the sun." -- LOGOS's syntax is clearest while remaining faithful to the Hebrew. "Allotted" (EMET, LOGOS) is preferred over "assigned" (PNEUMA) for the Hebrew hippil. "Toward the setting of the sun" (EMET, LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew mevo' ha-shemesh rather than reducing it to "in the west" (PNEUMA). PNEUMA's "including every nation I have already destroyed" reinterprets "cut off" (hikratti) too freely.

---

### 23:5 -- "thrust them out" vs. "push them out" (yehdof)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he shall thrust them out from before you, and he shall drive them out from before you" |
| LOGOS | "he shall thrust them out from before you and shall drive them out from before you" |
| PNEUMA | "will himself push them out ahead of you and drive them from your path" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "he shall thrust them out from before you and shall drive them out from before you" -- EMET and LOGOS agree closely. "Thrust" captures the violent force of the Hebrew hadaf better than PNEUMA's softer "push." PNEUMA's "from your path" replaces the repeated "from before you" (mi-peneikhem), losing the repetition that the Hebrew deliberately employs for emphasis.

---

### 23:6 -- "scroll" vs. "book" (sefer) and "Torah" vs. "Instruction" (torah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the scroll of the Torah of Moses" |
| LOGOS | "the book of the Torah of Moses" |
| PNEUMA | "the scroll of the Instruction of Moses" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the scroll of the Torah of Moses" -- For sefer: "scroll" (EMET, PNEUMA) reflects the historical medium accurately. For torah: "Torah" (EMET, LOGOS) is retained as a loanword that preserves the full semantic range. PNEUMA's "Instruction" is a valid translation of torah but narrows its meaning and loses recognition of the term's established usage.

---

### 23:6 -- "not turning from it to the right or to the left" vs. "never straying from it one way or the other"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "so as not to turn from it [to the] right or [to the] left" |
| LOGOS | "not turning from it to the right or to the left" |
| PNEUMA | "never straying from it one way or the other" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "not turning from it to the right or to the left" -- LOGOS's phrasing is adopted. The right-left idiom is a recognizable biblical formula for total fidelity (cf. Deut 5:32, 17:11, 28:14; Josh 1:7) and is fully comprehensible in English. PNEUMA's paraphrase erases this intertextual connection.

---

### 23:7 -- "invoke" vs. "bring to remembrance" (tazkiru)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the name of their gods you shall not bring to remembrance" |
| LOGOS | "the name of their gods you shall not invoke" |
| PNEUMA | "Do not invoke the names of their gods" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the name of their gods you shall not invoke" -- "Invoke" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) captures the Hiphil of zakhar (to cause to be remembered, to mention, to call upon) in a single word that conveys both the speech-act and its ritual significance. EMET's "bring to remembrance" is more literal but somewhat awkward. The Hebrew word order (object-before-verb) is preserved per EMET and LOGOS.

---

### 23:7 -- "these remaining ones with you" vs. "these that remain with you" vs. "that still remain among you"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "these remaining [ones] with you" |
| LOGOS | "these that remain with you" |
| PNEUMA | "that still remain among you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "these remaining ones with you" -- The Hebrew ha-nish'arim uses a Niphal participle functioning as an adjective. EMET's rendering is most literal. "Remaining ones" preserves the participial form as a substantive, which is how the Hebrew functions. This phrase recurs at vv. 7 and 12, and consistency matters.

---

### 23:8 -- "cling" vs. "hold fast" (tidbaqun)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall cling" |
| LOGOS | "you shall cling" |
| PNEUMA | "hold fast" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "you shall cling" -- EMET and LOGOS agree. The Hebrew davaq ("cling/cleave") is the same verb used for the marriage bond in Genesis 2:24, and "cling" preserves this important intertextual echo. PNEUMA's "hold fast" is close in meaning but loses the specific verbal connection.

---

### 23:10 -- "shall put a thousand to flight" vs. "pursues a thousand" vs. "can put a thousand to flight"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "One man of you pursues a thousand" |
| LOGOS | "One man of you shall put to flight a thousand" |
| PNEUMA | "A single one of you can put a thousand to flight" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "One man of you shall put a thousand to flight" -- The Hebrew yirdof is imperfect, expressing ongoing capacity. LOGOS's "shall put to flight" captures both the future/habitual aspect and the military meaning of radaf. EMET's "pursues" is too flat; PNEUMA's "can put" introduces a modal that the Hebrew does not contain.

---

### 23:11 -- "guard your souls diligently" vs. "guard your souls very much" vs. "be very careful... with your whole being"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall guard your souls very [much] to love YHWH your God" |
| LOGOS | "you shall guard your souls diligently, to love YHWH your God" |
| PNEUMA | "So be very careful to love YHWH your God with your whole being" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And you shall guard your souls diligently to love YHWH your God" -- LOGOS's rendering balances fidelity and readability. "Guard your souls" preserves nefesh in translation (EMET, LOGOS), while "diligently" renders me'od more naturally than "very much." PNEUMA's "with your whole being" is a valid interpretation of le-nafshoteikhem but restructures the clause.

---

### 23:12 -- Infinitive absolute: "turning, you turn back" vs. "indeed turn back" vs. "ever turn away" (shov tashuvu)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "if you shall indeed turn back" |
| LOGOS | "if you turn back at all" |
| PNEUMA | "if you ever turn away" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "if turning, you turn back" -- Per project convention for infinitive absolute constructions, the doubling is preserved with the English participial form. None of the three translators used this form here: EMET's "indeed" and LOGOS's "at all" weaken the Hebrew construction, while PNEUMA's "ever" obscures it entirely. The infinitive absolute shov tashuvu is a solemn rhetorical device and should be rendered with the characteristic doubling pattern.

---

### 23:12 -- "ally yourselves with" vs. "cling to" (davaqtem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you cling to the remnant" |
| LOGOS | "cling to the remnant" |
| PNEUMA | "ally yourselves with the remnant" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "you cling to the remnant" -- EMET and LOGOS preserve the Hebrew davaq, the same verb used positively in v. 8 for clinging to YHWH. The deliberate reuse of this verb creates a devastating irony: the clinging that should be directed to YHWH is misdirected to pagan nations. PNEUMA's "ally yourselves" erases this verbal echo.

---

### 23:13 -- Infinitive absolute: "knowing, you shall know" vs. "know for certain" (yado'a ted'u)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "knowing, you shall know" |
| LOGOS | "know for certain" |
| PNEUMA | "know for certain" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "knowing, you shall know" -- EMET alone preserves the infinitive absolute doubling. Per project convention, this construction is rendered with the English participial form to maintain the emphatic Hebrew pattern. This mirrors the treatment of shov tashuvu in v. 12, creating a consistent approach to the two infinitive absolutes in Joshua's warning.

---

### 23:13 -- "scourge" vs. "whip" (shotet)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a scourge on your sides" |
| LOGOS | "a scourge on your sides" |
| PNEUMA | "a whip on your sides" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "a scourge on your sides" -- EMET and LOGOS agree. "Scourge" carries a more severe, archaic force appropriate to covenantal curse language. "Whip" (PNEUMA) is adequate but slightly more commonplace. The Hebrew shotet denotes a lash or scourge used for punishment.

---

### 23:13 -- "perish" vs. "disappear" ('oved)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "until you perish from upon this good land" |
| LOGOS | "until you perish from upon this good land" |
| PNEUMA | "until you disappear from this good land" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "until you perish from upon this good land" -- EMET and LOGOS agree. The Hebrew 'avad means "to perish, be destroyed" — stronger than PNEUMA's "disappear," which lacks the finality of covenantal judgment. The phrase "from upon" (me'al) is also preserved, as it recurs in vv. 13, 15, and 16 as a fixed formula.

---

### 23:14 -- "has fallen" vs. "has failed" (nafal)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "not one word has fallen from all the good words" / "not one word from them has fallen" |
| LOGOS | "not one word has failed from all the good words" / "not one word of them has failed" |
| PNEUMA | "not a single one of all the good things... has gone unfulfilled" / "not one has failed" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "not one word has fallen from all the good words" / "not one word of them has fallen" -- The Hebrew nafal literally means "to fall." EMET alone preserves this concrete image. Words "fall" to the ground when they go unfulfilled; words that are fulfilled "stand." This is a vivid metaphor, comprehensible in English, and deserves preservation. LOGOS's "failed" and PNEUMA's "gone unfulfilled" interpret the metaphor rather than transmitting it.

---

### 23:14 -- "with all your heart and with all your soul" vs. "with every fiber of your being" vs. "deep down"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "with all your heart and with all your soul" |
| LOGOS | "in all your hearts and in all your souls" |
| PNEUMA | "deep down — with every fiber of your being" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "with all your heart and with all your soul" -- EMET's rendering preserves the Hebrew doublet be-khol levavkhem u-ve-khol nafshekem, which echoes the Shema (Deut 6:5) and the covenant language of Deuteronomy. This intertextual link is theologically critical in Joshua's farewell speech, which is saturated with Deuteronomic language. PNEUMA's consolidation erases this echo entirely.

---

### 23:15 -- "every evil word" vs. "every disaster he warned of" (kol ha-davar ha-ra)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "all the evil word" |
| LOGOS | "every evil word" |
| PNEUMA | "every disaster he warned of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "every evil word" -- The parallelism between "every good word" and "every evil word" in this verse is deliberate and structurally central to Joshua's argument. LOGOS's rendering with "every" (rather than EMET's "all the") best represents the Hebrew kol + singular noun in natural English. PNEUMA's "disaster he warned of" interprets the content rather than preserving the form of the parallel.

---

### 23:15 -- "destroyed" vs. "wiped you out" (hashmido)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "until he has destroyed you" |
| LOGOS | "until he has destroyed you" |
| PNEUMA | "until he has wiped you out" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "until he has destroyed you" -- EMET and LOGOS agree. The Hiphil of shamad ("to destroy, annihilate") is a standard Deuteronomic covenant-curse term (cf. Deut 4:26, 28:63). "Destroyed" preserves this formal register. PNEUMA's "wiped you out" is colloquial and diminishes the severity.

---

### 23:16 -- "anger of YHWH shall burn" vs. "YHWH's anger will blaze" (ve-charah af YHWH)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the anger of YHWH shall burn against you" |
| LOGOS | "the anger of YHWH shall burn against you" |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH's anger will blaze against you" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the anger of YHWH shall burn against you" -- EMET and LOGOS agree precisely. The Hebrew ve-charah af means "and the nose/anger shall burn hot." "Shall burn" is the standard rendering of charah and preserves the heat-of-anger metaphor. PNEUMA's "blaze" is close but introduces a different fire image. The possessive construction "the anger of YHWH" (EMET, LOGOS) follows the Hebrew construct chain more closely than "YHWH's anger" (PNEUMA).

---

### 23:16 -- "perish quickly" vs. "quickly vanish" (va-avadtem meherah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall perish quickly" |
| LOGOS | "you shall perish quickly" |
| PNEUMA | "you will quickly vanish" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "you shall perish quickly" -- EMET and LOGOS agree. As in v. 13, "perish" ('avad) is the correct rendering for covenantal destruction language. PNEUMA's "vanish" is too soft. The word order "perish quickly" follows the Hebrew verb-adverb sequence.

---
